content.full_text:
April 23, 1990

Kathy Donovan
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento 95804

Dear Kathy:

As you know we are preparing a major study on the initiative process in California. Part of the report includes a detailed examination of how campaigns supporting and opposing measures spend their funds.

We have been looking at the Proposition 104 campaign where the supporters spent millions of dollars and the opponents practically nothing. But when I talked to someone who watched the campaign carefully, he observed that the opponents had received at least one million dollars in free broadcast time under the Fairness Doctrine. As far as we can determine, none of this time was disclosed. I can understand why this should not be considered a contribution to the campaign or an expenditure by the broadcast companies; but I believe that somewhere on the campaign statement, the free time should be listed. The public should know how much free time is given.

I would appreciate a review of this question and perhaps consideration of providing advice to campaigns who receive such free media on where to report the time on their campaign statements. Even requiring an attached letter to the campaign statement explaining the what was received would be a step in the right direction.

Please call me if you have any questions or suggestions.

Best wishes,

Robert M. Stern
Co-Director
